```mermaid
    classDef ctlNode fill:#FBCFE8,stroke:#9D174D,color:#831843

    class MA,MC,MI,MB matNode
    class FB,FV,FI,FJ ferNode
    class O1,O2,O3,O4,O5,O6 outNode
    class C1,C2,C3,C4,CJ,CA,CP,CF ctlNode
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy of a Modern Industrial Fermenter Control System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Level 1: Field Sensors and Actuators] --> L2[Level 2: PLC and Data Acquisition]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3: SCADA Supervisory Control]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4: MES Manufacturing Execution]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5: ERP Business Planning]

    L1 --> S1[pH Probe]
    L1 --> S2[DO Probe]
    L1 --> S3[Temperature RTD]
    L1 --> S4[Pressure Transducer]
    L1 --> S5[Load Cell for Weight]
    L1 --> S6[Peristaltic Pumps]
    L1 --> S7[Solenoid Valves]

    L2 --> P1[PID Loop Controllers]
    L2 --> P2[Alarm Management]

    L3 --> SC1[HMI Operator Interface]
    L3 --> SC2[Batch Recipe Manager]
    L3 --> SC3[Trend Historian]

    classDef lvl1 fill:#BFDBFE,stroke:#1E40AF,color:#1E3A8A
    classDef lvl2 fill:#FDE68A,stroke:#B45309,color:#78350F
    classDef lvl3 fill:#FCA5A5,stroke:#991B1B,color:#7F1D1D
    classDef lvl4 fill:#C4B5FD,stroke:#5B21B6,color:#4C1D95
    classDef lvl5 fill:#A7F3D0,stroke:#047857,color:#064E3B

    class L1,S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7 lvl1
    class L2,P1,P2 lvl2
    class L3,SC1,SC2,SC3 lvl3
    class L4 lvl4
    class L5 lvl5
```

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Strategy:** In the 14-mark "draw and explain" question, draw the fermenter in **cross-section** (not as a block diagram). The Mermaid diagrams above are excellent for explaining **flow logic and control hierarchy** as a supplementary figure. Always label **at least 10 parts** to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a bioprocess fermenter. List any four essential components of a stirred tank fermenter.**

**Model Answer:**
A bioprocess fermenter (bioreactor) is a closed, aseptic vessel designed to provide a controlled environment — temperature, pH, dissolved oxygen, and agitation — for the growth of microorganisms or cells to produce a target biochemical product.

Four essential components of a stirred tank fermenter (STF) are:
1. **Vessel** (Stainless Steel SS-316L construction with aspect ratio 2:1 to 3:1).
2. **Agitation system** (Drive motor + shaft + Rushton disc turbine impellers).
3. **Aeration system** (Sparger + 0.2 µm air filter + exhaust line).
4. **Sensor and control system** (pH probe, DO probe, RTD temperature probe, foam sensor).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for components.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the standard sterilization-in-place (SIP) condition for a fermenter? Why is steam preferred over dry heat?**

**Model Answer:**
The **standard SIP condition is 121.1 °C at 15 psi (1.03 bar) gauge pressure for 20 to 30 minutes**, ensuring a 12-log reduction of *Geobacillus stearothermophilus* spores (the biological indicator).

Steam is preferred over dry heat because:
- **Latent heat of condensation** (2257 kJ/kg) delivers energy rapidly and at a lower temperature.
- **Moist heat denatures proteins** more efficiently than dry heat by hydrolyzing peptide bonds.
- Steam penetrates porous loads and crevices uniformly.
- It is more energy-efficient and gentler on stainless steel surfaces.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for condition, 2 marks for reasons.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, with two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **ONE full question** (either Q.A or Q.B). Both Q.A and Q.B are provided below as alternatives.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat labeled diagram, describe the construction and working of a **Stirred Tank Fermenter (STF)**. Explain the role of baffles, sparger, and impeller. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:** A stirred tank fermenter consists of a **cylindrical SS-316L vessel** with a dished top and bottom. The aspect ratio (H/D) is maintained at 2:1 to 3:1. A top-mounted motor drives a vertical shaft fitted with **two to four Rushton disc turbines**. **Four vertical baffles** are mounted on the inner wall. Sterile air is introduced through a **ring sparger** at the base. The vessel is jacketed for temperature control. Probes for pH, DO, temperature, and foam are mounted on the head plate.

**Working:** The vessel is filled to 70–80% working volume with sterile medium. After inoculation, the impeller rotates (typically **200–500 RPM**), creating turbulent mixing. Sterile air is sparged at a rate of **0.5–1.5 VVM** (volume of air per volume of medium per minute). The pH is maintained by automatic acid/base addition. Temperature is held at the setpoint (e.g., 30 °C for mesophilic cultures) by circulating chilled water through the jacket. The broth is harvested after the desired biomass or product concentration is reached.

**Role of Key Components:**
- **Baffles:** Four vertical strips prevent vortex formation, break tangential flow, and improve radial mixing and air dispersion.
- **Sparger:** Disperses incoming air into fine bubbles, maximizing the gas–liquid interfacial area for oxygen transfer.
- **Impeller:** Provides mechanical agitation to (i) suspend cells, (ii) disperse air bubbles, (iii) enhance heat transfer, and (iv) maintain homogeneous concentration of substrates.

*(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for working, 2 for component roles.)*

---

**(b)** What is the **volumetric mass transfer coefficient ($k_L a$)**? Derive the oxygen transfer rate equation using the **two-film theory**. State two methods to experimentally determine $k_L a$ in a fermenter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** The volumetric mass transfer coefficient $k_L a$ is the product of the liquid-side mass transfer coefficient ($k_L$) and the gas–liquid interfacial area per unit volume ($a$). It is a lumped parameter that quantifies the **aeration efficiency** of a fermenter, with units of $\text{h}^{-1}$ or $\text{s}^{-1}$.

**Derivation (Two-Film Theory):**
According to the two-film theory, the molar flux of oxygen across the stagnant liquid film surrounding an air bubble is given by Fick's First Law:

$$N_A = k_L \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

Multiplying both sides by the interfacial area per unit volume $a$:

$$\text{OTR} = N_A \cdot a = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

Here, $C^*$ is the saturation dissolved oxygen concentration and $C_L$ is the bulk dissolved oxygen concentration.

**Methods to Determine $k_L a$ Experimentally:**
1. **Dynamic Gassing-Out Method (Easy and Most Common):** The air supply is cut off, allowing DO to fall. When the air is re-introduced, the rate of DO recovery is monitored. The slope of the recovery curve gives $k_L a$.
2. **Sulfite Oxidation Method:** Sodium sulfite is oxidized to sulfate in the presence of a copper catalyst. The rate of oxygen consumption is measured chemically.
3. **Oxygen Balance / Mass Balance Method** in a real fermentation.

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for derivation, 2 for methods.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare the **Batch, Fed-Batch, and Continuous modes** of fermentation. Mention one industrial example for each. Explain why **Fed-Batch is preferred for recombinant protein production** in *E. coli*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | Batch | Fed-Batch | Continuous (CSTR) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Nutrient addition | All at t = 0 | Continuous/pulsed feeding | Continuous inflow + outflow |
| Product removal | End of cycle | End of cycle | Continuous |
| Operational state | Transient | Pseudo-steady | True steady state |
| Productivity | Lowest | High | Highest |
| Contamination risk | Moderate | Moderate | High |
| Industrial example | Penicillin (classic batch) | Recombinant Insulin in *E. coli* | Bioethanol, Single-Cell Protein |

**Why Fed-Batch is Preferred for Recombinant Protein in *E. coli*:**
1. **Avoidance of Catabolite Repression:** Excess glucose represses the lac operon and many recombinant promoters. Slow feeding of glucose keeps it at a concentration (< 0.5 g/L) that does not trigger repression.
2. **Avoidance of Overflow Metabolism (Acetate Formation):** High initial glucose leads to acetate accumulation, which is toxic to *E. coli* and inhibits growth and protein synthesis.
3. **Achievement of High Cell Density:** Fed-batch can achieve OD₆₀₀ > 100, far exceeding batch culture (OD₆₀₀ ≈ 5–10).
4. **Optimal Plasmid Stability:** Controlled growth rates reduce the burden of plasmid maintenance, improving the fraction of cells that retain the expression vector.

*(7 marks: 3 for comparison, 2 for examples, 2 for fed-batch rationale.)*

---

**(b)** List and briefly explain **any four sensor-probe systems** used in a fermenter. What is a **PID controller** and how does it maintain a setpoint? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Four Sensor-Probe Systems:**
1. **pH Probe (Combined Glass Electrode):** A sterilizable electrode that measures the hydrogen ion activity. It generates a millivolt signal proportional to pH, which is fed back to a controller that actuates acid/base pumps to maintain the setpoint (typically 6.8–7.2 for *E. coli*).
2. **Dissolved Oxygen (DO) Probe:** A polarographic (Clark-type) electrode with a gas-permeable PTFE membrane. It measures the partial pressure of oxygen dissolved in the broth. Used in a cascade loop to control agitation RPM or air flow rate.
3. **Temperature Probe (RTD/PT-100):** A resistance temperature detector whose resistance changes linearly with temperature. Connected to a PID controller that modulates the flow of chilled water or steam through the jacket.
4. **Foam Sensor (Conductivity Probe):** A dual-electrode probe mounted in the headspace. When foam rises and bridges the two electrodes, conductivity increases, triggering the antifoam pump.
5. *(Bonus: Redox Probe, Turbidity Probe, Capacitance Probe for biomass, Gas Analyzer for O₂/CO₂ in exhaust.)*

**PID Controller:**
A **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller** is a feedback control algorithm that calculates an error signal as the difference between the measured process variable (PV) and the desired setpoint (SP). It then outputs a corrective control action using three terms:

$$u(t) = K_P \cdot e(t) + K_I \int e(t) \, dt + K_D \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where:
- $K_P$, $K_I$, $K_D$ are the proportional, integral, and derivative gains.
- $e(t) = SP - PV$ is the instantaneous error.

**Working:** The **P term** provides an immediate response proportional to the current error. The **I term** eliminates steady-state offset by accumulating past errors. The **D term** anticipates future error by considering the rate of change, damping overshoot. Together, the PID loop rapidly drives the process variable to the setpoint and holds it there, which is critical for tight control of pH, temperature, and DO.

*(7 marks: 4 for sensors, 3 for PID.)*

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully Before Exam:**
> 1. **Confusing "Fermenter" with "Fermentation":** A fermenter is the *vessel/reactor*; fermentation is the *biological process*. Examiners deduct marks for this mix-up.
> 2. **Forgetting the Sterile Air Filter:** Every aeration line MUST have a **0.2 µm hydrophobic filter** both at the inlet AND at the exhaust. Skipping this in a diagram loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Using $\vert$ symbol inside answer tables:** While writing, avoid the LaTeX vertical pipe for absolute value, as it can confuse the row-rendering in some exam-booklet printouts. Use descriptive forms like $(C^* - C_L)$.
> 4. **No diagram in a 14-mark question:** Even a hand-drawn, slightly imperfect cross-sectional diagram is worth **at least 2–3 marks**. Never skip it.
> 5. **Memorizing OTR Equation Without Units:** Always state "$k_L a$" has units of $\text{h}^{-1}$ and "OTR" has units of $\text{mol} \cdot \text{L}^{-1} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$. Marks are lost for missing units.
> 6. **Writing $C^*$ as $C$ star instead of $C$-superscript-star in LaTeX:** Use $C^{*}$ (asterisk as superscript) in answer sheets to avoid confusion with the saturation notation.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Fermenter = Bioreactor = Closed Aseptic Vessel** for controlled biological reactions.
- [x] **Standard materials:** SS-316L stainless steel, glass (for lab-scale).
- [x] **Aspect Ratio (H/D):** 2:1 to 3:1 for stirred tanks.
- [x] **Working Volume:** 70–80% of total vessel.
- [x] **Four Baffles** is the engineering optimum.
- [x] **Rushton Disc Turbine** is the most common impeller for aerobic low-viscosity cultures.
- [x] **Sparger** at the bottom for air dispersion; **exhaust** at the top.
- [x] **SIP condition:** **121.1 °C, 15 psi, 20 min** — memorize verbatim.
- [x] **CIP = Cleaning-In-Place** (typically with NaOH, HNO₃, and hot water rinses).
- [x] **$k_L a > 200 \text{ h}^{-1}$** is the industrial benchmark for highly aerobic cultures.
- [x] **OTR = $k_L a (C^* - C_L)$** — guaranteed 14-mark derivation question.
- [x] **Power Number $N_P \approx 5.5$** for a Rushton turbine in fully turbulent flow.
- [x] **PID Controller** is the heart of fermenter automation.
- [x] **Fed-batch** is preferred for recombinant proteins to avoid catabolite repression and acetate toxicity.
- [x] **Continuous (CSTR) cultivation** uses a chemostat where dilution rate = specific growth rate at steady state.
- [x] **Scale-up criteria** include constant $P/V$, constant $k_L a$, constant impeller tip speed, or constant mixing time.
- [x] **DO cascade** automatically increases RPM when DO falls below setpoint.
- [x] **Antifoam control** is automatic; common antifoams are **silicone-based (e.g., polydimethylsiloxane)** or **polypropylene glycol (PPG)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Word:** This is a **lab-oriented** module in the KTU 2024 scheme. The questions you will face are rarely pure theory — they expect you to **link a component on the fermenter to a measurable parameter and to a control strategy**. Whenever you answer, always structure it as: **"Component → Function → Measured Variable → Controller → Output."** This five-link chain is exactly how KTU board examiners award full marks.
